cards.title: Why funnels beat a single sales ad
cards.front: Why not just run one ad that asks for the sale?
cards.back: A funnel exists to transform social users who are unaware of the problem AND your brand into people who are aware of the problem, aware of the solution, and have intent to purchase. One ad can't do all that — the structural job of a funnel is to move people across those awareness stages before asking for the sale.
cards.example: A cold scroller meets an educational video, then a lead magnet, then a sales page — instead of a buy-now ad they'd ignore.

cards.title: Facebook Flight Plan — 3 awareness stages
cards.front: What three stages does the Facebook Flight Plan move people through?
cards.back:
The Facebook Flight Plan is a perpetual 'aeronautical chart' system you build once and let run, moving people through three stages:
- Completely UNAWARE of the problem the product solves
- Fully AWARE of the problem and having intent to solve it
- Having INTENT to want to buy YOUR specific product or service

Along the way you perpetually build awareness, fans, leads/customers, and goodwill.

cards.title: FANS system
cards.front: What is the 'FANS system' you build on top of the Flight Plan?
cards.back: FANS is the named perpetual machine built on the Facebook Flight Plan: continuously building Awareness, Fans, leads/customers, and goodwill with your audience along the way. The end goal is an automated customer-acquisition machine that turns users who are unaware of the problem and brand into people aware of the problem, aware of the solution, and with intent to purchase.

cards.title: UPSYD retargeting / awareness ladder
cards.front: In the UPSYD ladder, what does the 'D' rung do for hot prospects?
cards.back:
UPSYD is the awareness-to-purchase ladder used in Stage Three retargeting. The captured rung is:
- D = Deal / buying incentive — nudge hesitant hot prospects with a bonus, discount, or expiration/scarcity.

After an educational video ad, prospects can move from unawareness all the way up the UPSYD ladder.
cards.example: "Get a 40 percent discount if you purchase by tomorrow!"

cards.title: Six Proven Facebook-Friendly Offers
cards.front: What are the 6 proven Facebook offer types, ordered least to most commitment?
cards.back:
Six offer types proven after $27.7M in agency ad spend, in reverse order of commitment (1 = least, 6 = most):
- 1. Coupon, Sweepstakes, or Blog
- 2. Checklist, Swipe File, or Cheat Sheet Download
- 3. Quiz / Survey Funnel
- 4. Webinar or Event
- 5. Free + Shipping for Physical Products
- 6. Product Sales Page

Beginners should start with just ONE offer for momentum; match the right offer to the right ad type.

cards.title: Beginners: launch with ONE offer
cards.front: How many offers should a beginner launch on their first campaign?
cards.back: Start with just ONE offer to get initial momentum. Intermediate/advanced advertisers who are scaling may use several offers — but always match the right offer with the right ad type.

cards.title: eCommerce Funnel 1 — Lead Magnet to Sales
cards.front: What's the proven eCommerce funnel that warms leads before the sale?
cards.back:
eCommerce Funnel 1 — Lead Magnet to Sales:
- Facebook VIDEO ad
- Basic squeeze-page lead magnet (e.g., one-day detox)
- Product sales page
- Order form

Video gets quality (brand-aware, warm) leads so the transition to sale is easier. After opt-in, a video acknowledges their action, gives access directions, thanks them, guides usage, then transitions into why your product is the next best step.
cards.example: Video ad → one-day detox opt-in → green-juice sales page → order form.

cards.title: eCommerce Funnel 2 — Direct to Sales
cards.front: Can you take a cold audience straight to a sales page with no lead magnet?
cards.back:
Yes — eCommerce Funnel 2 — Direct to Sales:
- Facebook ad
- LONG-FORM sales page
- Order form (no lead magnet, one step further down the funnel)

With the right messaging (audience's frustration, challenge, deep desires), a cold audience can go straight to the sales page. The long-form page has product info, testimonials (trust), a desire-building video, and order-form buttons.

cards.title: Info Funnel 1 — Lead Magnet + Tripwire + Core + Upsell
cards.front: What's the classic info-product funnel with a tripwire?
cards.back:
Info/Digital Funnel 1:
- Facebook LINK-POST ad offering a valuable lead magnet
- Lead-magnet opt-in
- Tripwire
- Core offer
- Upsell

Works in big B2C niches (fitness, passionate niches) and B2B. (Tripwire is usually under $20 — a low-priced impulse buy, term coined by Digital Marketer.)

cards.title: Info Funnel 2 — Webinar / Live Event
cards.front: Which funnel is ideal for high-priced products, and why?
cards.back:
Info/Digital Funnel 2 — Webinar / Live Event:
- Facebook VIDEO ad (bigger commitment)
- Webinar/live-event registration page
- Thank-you page

Ideal for high-priced products because you get more time with prospects.

cards.title: Local/Retail/Service Funnel — Discount to Landing Page
cards.front: What's the simplest funnel for a local business, and where do you start?
cards.back: Local/Retail/Service Funnels 1 & 2 — Discount/Incentive to Landing Page: a website-conversion campaign driving traffic to a landing page with a coupon or giveaway. Same funnel, two versions — one uses a VIDEO ad, the other an IMAGE-BASED LINK-POST ad (the recommended starting point: keep it simple). Coupon ideas: 7 days free at a gym, BOGO, free consultation. People share coupons, broadening reach.

cards.title: Offer #1 — Coupon / Sweepstakes / Blog
cards.front: Do you need a video ad to run a coupon offer?
cards.back: No. Offer #1 (least commitment) coupons like '$30 off' / '$50 off' work wonders for local businesses. Deliver via VIDEO ad → coupon landing page OR SHORT-COPY ad → coupon offer (video NOT required; both work equally well). Hidden benefit: organic brand exposure plus a subconscious brand-to-problem association.
cards.example: Eagle Leather repurposed a 30-second local TV spot as a Facebook video ad → coupon landing page and it crushed it.

cards.title: Short-Copy Ad to Ungated Blog Post
cards.front: What's the author's favorite campaign type that never pitches?
cards.back: Short-Copy Ad to Ungated Blog Post — drive cold traffic to free 'ungated' content (no opt-in) to indoctrinate the brand without pitching and feed a remarketing campaign. A long-term goodwill play that also works short-term. Success hinges on the right HOOK and requires proper tracking (e.g., Wicked Reports) to identify and promote best-performing content.

cards.title: Offer #2 — Checklist / Swipe File / Cheat Sheet
cards.front: How do you structure a checklist/cheat-sheet download offer?
cards.back: Offer #2: SHORT-COPY ad → simple opt-in landing page to download a tool that gets a result. Example: 'The 7-Step Facebook Campaign Checklist' (copy slightly longer because a webinar reg page follows). Digital Marketer's 'The Ultimate Social Media Swipe File' runs as a DISPLAY ad (not just newsfeed) and does incredibly well in both placements.

cards.title: Offer #3 — Quiz / Survey Funnel
cards.front: Why does a quiz funnel work even on people who never buy?
cards.back: Offer #3: curiosity-driven hook via LINK-POST ad → quiz/survey landing page. It works like a doctor's prescription: branch logic, tailored to the person, ending in an answer-based solution. Opt-in is required to see results; the prescription can be free or paid. Non-buyers still hand you data on the avatar's fears/desires for crafting hooks.

cards.title: Offer #4 — Webinar / Event (pre-commit with long copy)
cards.front: Why do webinar ads need long copy, and how do you structure it?
cards.back:
A webinar asks for 90–120 minutes, so pre-commit prospects with LONG COPY. Proven method: a 30-second clip with a text-overlay hook, then elaborate in long copy. Copy method:
- Restate the hook
- Make them aware of a problem
- Deliver on the hook's promise (e.g., reveal 'the three biggest mistakes')
- Invite to register for the free web class

Each step SEEDS the next (read → register → show up).
cards.example: "Game Changing Facebook Changes" — deliver the three biggest mistakes, then invite to a free web class.

cards.title: Offer #5 — Free + Shipping (physical)
cards.front: How can paying for your own leads still make economic sense?
cards.back: Offer #5 — Free + Shipping: the customer pays only shipping, lowering the barrier for cold audiences and converting prospects into qualified buyers — justified by a backend value ladder. Method: a ~10-minute video taught the #1 lesson from $600k/month avg ad spend, delivered the hook by teaching the four targeting groups to start every campaign with, then invited them to buy the book. Content delivering on the hook matters more than length. They pay for the leads (book bought from publisher) but it makes sense given value-ladder ascension.

cards.title: Offer #6 — Product Sales Page (educational video ad)
cards.front: What's the most powerful ad type for cold traffic — and what must you NEVER do with it?
cards.back: Offer #6 — Product Sales Page via an educational video ad is the most powerful ad type. To cold traffic, give value upfront — NEVER run a straight CTA-to-buy video ('there has to be a whole lot more to it than just a CTA'). EDUCATE to build authority/credibility/rapport, then pivot to the offer → long-form sales page (top-of-fold hook restates benefits; page restates the video). Implement the three-step video ad formula (Ch. 22).
cards.example: Drew Canole's "11 Superfoods" video educates, then pivots to Organifi green juice → long-form sales page.

cards.title: Splintering Your Offer
cards.front: How do you turn a big product into an irresistible front-end offer?
cards.back: Splinter off an easily consumable, highly actionable, highly valuable, popular chunk of your main product as the front-end offer for cold traffic. Give away your best stuff, but keep it INCOMPLETE so they want more.
cards.example: A 7-lesson 'How to learn rock guitar' course → most-popular riffs lesson → splinter off just '5 killer licks.'

cards.title: Ad Scent / Ad-to-Landing-Page Congruency
cards.front: What silently kills conversions even when your ad gets great clicks?
cards.back: Weak Ad Scent — the visual/message continuity between an ad and its landing page. Match the ad copy, headline, and even color scheme to the hook; congruency from ad to landing page is essential for conversions. Weak ad scent kills conversions even with strong clicks. It's easy to overlook and easy to fix — check that the ad and landing page clearly belong together.
cards.example: Landing-page headline matches the ad hook "When Is the Best Time to Send Your Emails?"

cards.title: Work Backward From the End Goal to the Hook
cards.front: Where do you actually START when designing a hook and offer?
cards.back: Define the ultimate offer/end goal FIRST, then work backward through the offer to the hook. Each step (hook → free offer → paid offer) must be a logical, INCOMPLETE progression toward the core product — typically what the prospect NEEDS, not just what they want.

cards.title: Find a Solution — craft the hook & offer
cards.front: What's the checklist for crafting a hook and offer from market research?
cards.back:
Find a Solution checklist:
- Review notes on the market's desires and pain points
- Force-rank those pain points and desires
- Separate WANTS from NEEDS — they are not the same
- Devise solutions the HOOK will promise and the OFFER will fulfill
- Ensure the offer fulfills the hook's promise but stays INCOMPLETE (bigger solution lives in the paid product)
- Work backward: define end-goal/core offer first, then offer, then hook
- Confirm each step (hook → free offer → paid offer) is a logical, incomplete progression toward the core product

cards.title: Ad-to-landing-page congruency check
cards.front: What four things confirm your ad and landing page belong together?
cards.back:
Ad-to-landing-page congruency check:
- Landing-page headline matches the ad's hook
- Ad copy and landing-page copy are aligned ('ad scent')
- Color schemes match to reinforce congruency
- Ad and landing page clearly look like they belong together

cards.title: Always use a thank-you page
cards.front: Why must every coupon/giveaway funnel now have a thank-you page?
cards.back:
Always use a thank-you page:
- Build a thank-you page (Facebook no longer keeps coupon redemption on-platform)
- For giveaways, place a coupon/offer on the thank-you page to filter lead quality

This separates freebie-seekers from genuine prospects and gives an incentive to call or come in.

cards.title: Landing-page conversion benchmarks
cards.front: What landing-page conversion rate is healthy, and what signals a problem?
cards.back:
Landing-page conversion benchmarks:
- ~20% = average reference benchmark for a landing page
- < 10% = you have a problem — split test and rework page/registration/sales pages

cards.title: Offer & price thresholds
cards.front: What are the key price/time thresholds for tripwires, webinars, and offsets?
cards.back:
Offer & price thresholds:
- Tripwire price: usually < $20 (low-priced impulse buy, coined by Digital Marketer)
- Webinar time ask: 90–120 minutes (why webinar copy must pre-commit)
- High-ticket webinar threshold: $500+ (price point where webinars warm cold traffic)
- Thank-you-page offset offer: ~$50 product to offset ad cost
- Coupon value examples: $30 off / $50 off (local businesses)
- Six-offer ad-spend basis: $27.7M

cards.title: Buying-incentive retargeting copy (the 'D')
cards.front: What does retargeting copy that nudges a hot, hesitant prospect look like?
cards.back:
Buying-incentive (the 'D' in UPSYD) — nudge hot prospects with a bonus, discount, or expiration/scarcity. Templates:
- "Get a 40 percent discount if you purchase by tomorrow!"
- "Get a free tote bag with purchase of any performance gear! (Value $25. Hurry—offer ends soon!)"

cards.title: Hook revision — incomplete beats give-it-all-away
cards.front: Which hook generated thousands of leads and zero buyers — and what fixed it?
cards.back:
House-flipping hook revision:
- OLD (gives everything away): "How to flip houses in five simple steps" → 70-page eBook → thousands of cheap leads, ZERO buyers
- NEW (incomplete, sells the need): "17 words you need to say to an investor to fund your next deal" → 3-page checklist → far more purchases

Lesson: the hook should promise a specific, incomplete result the paid product completes.

cards.title: Low-friction lead magnet / tripwire copy
cards.front: What does a low-friction text-to-opt-in trigger into a tripwire sound like?
cards.back:
Text-to-opt-in trigger → cheap high-value next step → benefit-driven promise:

"And check this out—if you text 'guitar master' to 555-555-play, I'll send you a cool 'Five-day guitar boot camp for $20' coupon that one of my best instructors puts on every week. He shows you even more tips, tricks, and secrets to getting great at the guitar fast."

cards.title: Don't give away the whole solution (Incompleteness Principle)
cards.front: Why did a generous 70-page eBook lead magnet produce zero buyers?
cards.back: A 'how to flip houses in five simple steps' 70-page eBook generated thousands of cheap leads but ZERO buyers — because the paid 8-module course taught the same thing. The offer must fulfill the hook's promise yet stay INCOMPLETE so the paid product delivers the bigger solution. The goal of a lead isn't a lead — it's a buyer. (Exception: it works if splintered as one hook among a full portfolio of products.)

cards.title: "Give me your card" is the polite no
cards.front: Why is ending with 'give me your card and I'll check it out' a lost lead?
cards.back: A high-friction, you-centric pitch ending with 'give me your card and I'll check it out' is a soft rejection, not a lead. If the funnel relies on the prospect remembering to find you later, you've lost them. Engineer immediate, easy action at peak interest instead.

cards.title: Don't send cold traffic to a catalog or bare coupon
cards.front: Why does a plain link-post ad to all your products fail on cold traffic?
cards.back: Running a regular link-post ad to all products, or a coupon with no rapport, interest, or desire messaging, fails on cold traffic. First raise awareness of the audience's frustration, challenge, and deep desires — don't lead with the catalog.

cards.title: Don't run a straight CTA-to-buy video to cold traffic
cards.front: What happens if you run a pure 'buy now' video to a cold audience?
cards.back: It won't work — 'there has to be a whole lot more to it than just a CTA.' Educate and deliver value upfront before asking cold traffic to buy.

cards.title: Webinar registration is only half the battle
cards.front: Why isn't a webinar signup the finish line?
cards.back: Even a free webinar is a big ask; your best prospects value time as much as money. Registration is only part of the battle — they must register, SHOW UP, AND actually consume the content. Funnels designed only to maximize signups under-deliver.

cards.title: Bow Tie Formula — sophisticated vs complicated
cards.front: What's the difference between a 'complicated' funnel and a 'sophisticated' one?
cards.back:
BlitzMetrics' Bow Tie Formula:
- COMPLICATED = tons of remarketing (custom), lookalike, and interest/behavior audiences spread across many ad sets
- SOPHISTICATED = most campaigns driven by REMARKETING instead of static demographic/interest targets

A powerful funnel has many SEQUENCES (IF/THEN statements). More touches = more chances to build value, raising user LTV and letting you spend more to acquire leads.

cards.title: Confusing sophisticated with complicated
cards.front: Does piling on more demographic/interest ad sets make a funnel sophisticated?
cards.back: No — piling on tons of demographic/interest ad sets is COMPLICATED, not sophisticated. Sophistication comes from remarketing-driven sequences (the Bow Tie Formula).

cards.title: Value, Value, Value, Offer
cards.front: How do you make a funnel feel like a friend's recommendation, not selling?
cards.back: Deliver value repeatedly before making an offer (Value, Value, Value, Offer) so the funnel generates goodwill and feels like a trusted friend's recommendation rather than selling. Combined with 'thinking like journalists,' articles gain authority that earns placement on high-profile sites, amplified via switch-boosting to media-inception targets.

cards.title: Switch-boost authority content to remarketing audiences
cards.front: How do you overcome objections during a long B2B consideration phase?
cards.back: Set triggers at conversion parts of the funnel to switch-boost authority content to remarketing audiences. For B2B or long consideration phases, surfacing high-authority mentions to remarketing lifts conversion by overcoming objections. It's also usually more powerful to boost earned third-party publications/quotes/mentions than your own site — OK to send ad dollars to someone else's site if the content is good.
